title: Frequentist Modeling of Immunoassay Standard Curves with the StandardCurve Class: ELISA Example
text: vignette demonstrates complete workflow fitting standard curves immunoassay data, including ELISA data, deriving detection metrics using curveRfreq package. workflow encapsulated StandardCurve R6 class, wraps full curveRfreq pipeline single stateful object clean, verb-driven API: class handles everything antigen selection model fitting, QC metrics, error propagation, plotting. walk complete workflow: Load prepare data — load built-elisa_assay_example dataset construct StandardCurve object study settings antigen constraints. Set Curve Settings ($set_curve_settings()) — Resolves antigen-specific curve settings data loaded StandardCurve object, including lower asymptote handling (fix, constrained, etc.) blank variability estimates. Fit ($fit()) — fit candidate nonlinear models constants set object select best model grounded information theory. Compare ($compare_models()) — Provides visual comparison converging models fit including overlay, residuals vs. fitted values, parameter estimates, AIC. Inflection Point Limits Detection (LOD) Minimum/Maximum Detectable Concentrations (MDC) Reliable Detection Limits (RDL) Curvature-based Limits Quantification (LOQ) Plot ($plot()) — visualize final standard curve Propagate error ($propagate_error()) — compute sample concentration predictions, measurement error, Precision Profile Extract ($get_results()) — pull tables downstream use

title: Frequentist Modeling of Immunoassay Standard Curves with the StandardCurve Class: ELISA Example
text: package ships elisa_assay_example, synthetic multi-plate ELISA immunoassay dataset containing standard curves, blanks, patient samples across one antigen (alpha) run six plates. structure elisa_assay_example: named list simulated multi-plate ELISA data single analyte (alpha), spanning 6 plates 20 patient samples per plate across 3 timepoints (baseline, month3, month6) 2 treatment groups. Plates 1–3 use five-parameter logistic (5-PL) standard curve; plates 4–6 use Gompertz curve, reflecting realistic -plate variability curve shape. assay response column od can also absorbance. assay_response_variable string corresponding nae assay response column. information can found running help(elisa_assay_example). StandardCurve class expects contain $standards, $blanks, $samples, $response_var, $indep_var. choose curve_id lookup table use custom filtering function (filter_by_curve_id) subset dataset interest (elisa_assay_example vignette). function takes full dataset chosen curve_id, filters relevant components, returns reduced dataset containing records associated specific curve. filtered dataset passed constructor.
code: data(elisa_assay_example)  # Inspect the curve/plate lookup table head(elisa_assay_example$curve_id_lookup) #>   curve_id antigen study_accession experiment_accession   plate #> 1        1   alpha SDYELISAexample           EXPexample plate_1 #> 2        2   alpha SDYELISAexample           EXPexample plate_2 #> 3        3   alpha SDYELISAexample           EXPexample plate_3 #> 4        4   alpha SDYELISAexample           EXPexample plate_4 #> 5        5   alpha SDYELISAexample           EXPexample plate_5 #> 6        6   alpha SDYELISAexample           EXPexample plate_6 str(elisa_assay_example, max.level = 2, give.attr = FALSE, vec.len = 2) #> List of 6 #>  $ standards      :'data.frame': 60 obs. of  8 variables: #>   ..$ curve_id                  : int [1:60] 1 1 1 1 1 ... #>   ..$ stype                     : chr [1:60] "S" "S" ... #>   ..$ sampleid                  : chr [1:60] "STD_01" "STD_02" ... #>   ..$ well                      : chr [1:60] "A1" "B1" ... #>   ..$ dilution                  : num [1:60] 1000 333 ... #>   ..$ od                        : num [1:60] 0.0955 0.0865 ... #>   ..$ assay_response_variable   : chr [1:60] "od" "od" ... #>   ..$ assay_independent_variable: chr [1:60] "concentration" "concentration" ... #>  $ blanks         :'data.frame': 24 obs. of  7 variables: #>   ..$ curve_id                  : int [1:24] 1 1 1 1 2 ... #>   ..$ stype                     : chr [1:24] "B" "B" ... #>   ..$ well                      : chr [1:24] "G11" "H11" ... #>   ..$ dilution                  : int [1:24] 1 1 1 1 1 ... #>   ..$ od                        : num [1:24] 0.0514 0.0483 0.0516 0.0415 0.0443 ... #>   ..$ assay_response_variable   : chr [1:24] "od" "od" ... #>   ..$ assay_independent_variable: chr [1:24] "concentration" "concentration" ... #>  $ samples        :'data.frame': 120 obs. of  12 variables: #>   ..$ curve_id                  : int [1:120] 1 1 1 1 1 ... #>   ..$ timeperiod                : chr [1:120] "baseline" "baseline" ... #>   ..$ patientid                 : chr [1:120] "PAT_001" "PAT_002" ... #>   ..$ well                      : chr [1:120] "A3" "B3" ... #>   ..$ stype                     : chr [1:120] "X" "X" ... #>   ..$ sampleid                  : chr [1:120] "a001" "a002" ... #>   ..$ agroup                    : chr [1:120] "GroupA" "GroupB" ... #>   ..$ dilution                  : int [1:120] 400 400 400 400 400 ... #>   ..$ samplingerrors            : num [1:120] NA NA NA NA NA ... #>   ..$ od                        : num [1:120] 2.77 3.01 ... #>   ..$ assay_response_variable   : chr [1:120] "od" "od" ... #>   ..$ assay_independent_variable: chr [1:120] "concentration" "concentration" ... #>  $ curve_id_lookup:'data.frame': 6 obs. of  5 variables: #>   ..$ curve_id            : int [1:6] 1 2 3 4 5 ... #>   ..$ antigen             : chr [1:6] "alpha" "alpha" ... #>   ..$ study_accession     : chr [1:6] "SDYELISAexample" "SDYELISAexample" ... #>   ..$ experiment_accession: chr [1:6] "EXPexample" "EXPexample" ... #>   ..$ plate               : chr [1:6] "plate_1" "plate_2" ... #>  $ response_var   : chr "od" #>  $ indep_var      : chr "concentration" filter_by_curve_id <- function(loaded_data,                                curve_id,                                target_names = c("standards", "blanks",                                                 "samples", "curve_id_lookup"),                                verbose = FALSE) {      filtered <- loaded_data      filtered$curve_id_whole_lookup <- filtered$curve_id_lookup   filtered$whole_standards <- filtered$standards   filtered[target_names] <- lapply(filtered[target_names], function(df) {          # Skip non-data frames     if (!is.data.frame(df)) {       if (verbose) message("[filter_by_curve_id] Skipping non-data.frame")       return(df)     }          # Skip empty data     if (nrow(df) == 0) {       if (verbose) message("[filter_by_curve_id] Empty data.frame")       return(df)     }          # Skip if no curve_id column     if (!"curve_id" %in% names(df)) {       if (verbose) message("[filter_by_curve_id] No curve_id column")       return(df)     }          # Filter     df[as.character(df$curve_id) == as.character(curve_id), , drop = FALSE]   })      return(filtered) } curve_id   <- 6 antigen <- elisa_assay_example$curve_id_lookup[elisa_assay_example$curve_id_lookup$curve_id == curve_id,]$antigen curve_data <- filter_by_curve_id(elisa_assay_example, curve_id = curve_id)

title: Frequentist Modeling of Immunoassay Standard Curves with the StandardCurve Class: ELISA Example
text: Parameters entire study set named list fitting standard curve influence fit. following parameters accepted values definitions. example uses suggested defaults. applyProzone Logical (TRUE FALSE). Determines whether prozone (hook effect) detection correction applied. TRUE: Detects adjusts potential signal suppression low dilutions. FALSE: prozone correction performed. blank_option Character string. Controls blank wells handled. Options include: "ignored": Blank wells excluded analysis. "subtracted": geometric mean blank responses subtracted observed responses. "subtracted_3x": 3 times geometric mean blank responses subtracted observed responses. "subtracted_10x": 10 times geometric mean blank responses subtracted observed responses. MFI values test samples zero possible (fluorescence intensity physically constrained non-negative values) set zero. is_log_response Logical (TRUE FALSE). Indicates whether response variable (e.g., MFI absorbance) log-transformed. TRUE: Fit models log-transformed response. FALSE: Use raw response values. is_log_independent Logical (TRUE FALSE). Specifies whether independent variable (concentration) log-transformed. TRUE: Use log-scale independent variable. FALSE: Use original scale.
code: study_params <- list(   applyProzone       = TRUE,   blank_option       = "ignored",   is_log_response    = TRUE,   is_log_independent = TRUE )

what: A more advanced example: Fitting across Multiple Plates
title: Frequentist Modeling of Immunoassay Standard Curves with the StandardCurve Class: ELISA Example
text: Since elisa_assay_data contains one antigen across six plates, can iterate combinations. Call filter_by_curve_id() new curve_id lookup table pass result $set_data() — replaces data clears downstream state automatically. Key Notes: Pull antigen name lookup table iteration know constraint set apply. assumes curve_id_lookup antigen column. Swap sc$antigen_constraints $set_curve_settings() set_curve_settings() reads antigen_constraints configure model bounds. swap , new constraints take effect next call. $set_data() clears downstream state (fitted models, summaries, etc.) reset antigen_constraints, swap step still necessary persist safely overwritten next iteration. Alternatively, constraint differences substantial want cleaner separation, instantiate fresh StandardCurve object per antigen inside loop using StandardCurve$new() appropriate constraint data frame. avoids risk stale state slightly verbose.
code: # Define per-antigen constraints as a named list antigen_constraints_list <- list(   alpha = data.frame(     antigen                      = "alpha",     l_asy_min_constraint         = 0,     l_asy_max_constraint         = 0,     l_asy_constraint_method      = "default",     standard_curve_concentration = 10000,     pcov_threshold               = 15,     stringsAsFactors             = FALSE   ) )  all_summaries <- vector("list", nrow(elisa_assay_example$curve_id_lookup))  for (i in seq_len(nrow(elisa_assay_example$curve_id_lookup))) {    cid        <- elisa_assay_example$curve_id_lookup$curve_id[i]   antigen    <- elisa_assay_example$curve_id_lookup$antigen[i]   # pull antigen name   curve_data <- filter_by_curve_id(elisa_assay_example, curve_id = cid)    # Update the StandardCurve object with new data AND new constraints   sc$set_data(curve_data)   sc$antigen_constraints <- antigen_constraints_list[[antigen]]  # swap constraints    sc$set_curve_settings()$fit()$summarize()   all_summaries[[i]] <- sc$get_results()$best_fit_summary }  combined_summary <- do.call(rbind, all_summaries) combined_summary

title: Frequentist Modeling of Immunoassay Standard Curves with the StandardCurve Class: Bead Array Example
text: vignette demonstrates complete workflow fitting standard curves immunoassay data, including bead array assay data, deriving detection metrics using curveRfreq package. workflow encapsulated StandardCurve R6 class, wraps full curveRfreq pipeline single stateful object clean, verb-driven API: class handles everything antigen selection model fitting, QC metrics, error propagation, plotting. walk complete workflow: Load prepare data — load built-bead_assay_example dataset construct StandardCurve object study settings antigen constraints. Set Curve Settings ($set_curve_settings()) — Resolves antigen-specific curve settings data loaded StandardCurve object, including lower asymptote handling (fix, constrained, etc.) blank variability estimates. Fit ($fit()) — fit candidate nonlinear models constants set object select best model grounded information theory. Compare ($compare_models()) — Provides visual comparison converging models fit including overlay, residuals vs. fitted values, parameter estimates, AIC. Inflection Point Limits Detection (LOD) Minimum/Maximum Detectable Concentrations (MDC) Reliable Detection Limits (RDL) Curvature-based Limits Quantification (LOQ) Plot ($plot()) — visualize final standard curve Propagate error ($propagate_error()) — compute sample concentration predictions, measurement error, Precision Profile Extract ($get_results()) — pull tables downstream use

title: Frequentist Modeling of Immunoassay Standard Curves with the StandardCurve Class: Bead Array Example
text: package ships bead_assay_example, synthetic multi-plate bead-based immunoassay dataset containing standard curves, blanks, patient samples across two antigens (alpha beta) run three plates. structure bead_assay_example: named list simulated multi-plate bead-based immunoassay data spanning 6 plates across 2 analytes (alpha beta), measured 3 replicate plates. contains 20 patient samples per plate across 3 timepoints (baseline, month3, month6) 2 treatment groups. response variable median fluorescence intensity (mfi) rather optical density absorbance. assay_response_variable string corresponding name assay response column (mfi bead-based assays). information can found running help(bead_assay_example). StandardCurve class expects bead_assay_example list contain $standards, $blanks, $samples, $response_var, $indep_var. choose curve_id lookup table use custom filtering function (filter_by_curve_id) subset dataset interest (bead_assay_example vignette). function takes full dataset chosen curve_id, filters relevant components, returns reduced dataset containing records associated specific curve. filtered dataset passed constructor.
code: data(bead_assay_example)  # Inspect the curve/plate lookup table head(bead_assay_example$curve_id_lookup) #>   curve_id antigen study_accession experiment_accession   plate #> 1        1   alpha      SDYexample           EXPexample plate_1 #> 2        2   alpha      SDYexample           EXPexample plate_2 #> 3        3   alpha      SDYexample           EXPexample plate_3 #> 4        4    beta      SDYexample           EXPexample plate_1 #> 5        5    beta      SDYexample           EXPexample plate_2 #> 6        6    beta      SDYexample           EXPexample plate_3 str(bead_assay_example, max.level = 2, give.attr = FALSE, vec.len = 2) #> List of 6 #>  $ standards      :'data.frame': 60 obs. of  8 variables: #>   ..$ curve_id                  : int [1:60] 1 1 1 1 int [1:120] 2000 2000 2000 2000 2000 ... #>   ..$ pctaggbeads               : num [1:120] 2.49 1.92 3.44 3.7 1.15 ... #>   ..$ samplingerrors            : num [1:120] NA NA NA NA NA ... #>   ..$ mfi                       : num [1:120] 18323 19415 ... #>   ..$ assay_response_variable   : chr [1:120] "mfi" "mfi" ... #>   ..$ assay_independent_variable: chr [1:120] "concentration" "concentration" ... #>  $ curve_id_lookup:'data.frame': 6 obs. of  5 variables: #>   ..$ curve_id            : int [1:6] 1 2 3 4 5 ... #>   ..$ antigen             : chr [1:6] "alpha" "alpha" ... #>   ..$ study_accession     : chr [1:6] "SDYexample" "SDYexample" ... #>   ..$ experiment_accession: chr [1:6] "EXPexample" "EXPexample" ... #>   ..$ plate               : chr [1:6] "plate_1" "plate_2" ... #>  $ response_var   : chr "mfi" #>  $ indep_var      : chr "concentration" filter_by_curve_id <- function(loaded_data,                                curve_id,                                target_names = c("standards", "blanks",                                                 "samples", "curve_id_lookup"),                                verbose = FALSE) {      filtered <- loaded_data      filtered$curve_id_whole_lookup <- filtered$curve_id_lookup   filtered$whole_standards <- filtered$standards   filtered[target_names] <- lapply(filtered[target_names], function(df) {          # Skip non-data frames     if (!is.data.frame(df)) {       if (verbose) message("[filter_by_curve_id] Skipping non-data.frame")       return(df)     }          # Skip empty data     if (nrow(df) == 0) {       if (verbose) message("[filter_by_curve_id] Empty data.frame")       return(df)     }          # Skip if no curve_id column     if (!"curve_id" %in% names(df)) {       if (verbose) message("[filter_by_curve_id] No curve_id column")       return(df)     }          # Filter     df[as.character(df$curve_id) == as.character(curve_id), , drop = FALSE]   })      return(filtered) } curve_id   <- 6 antigen <- bead_assay_example$curve_id_lookup[bead_assay_example$curve_id_lookup$curve_id == curve_id,]$antigen curve_data <- filter_by_curve_id(bead_assay_example, curve_id = curve_id)

title: Frequentist Modeling of Immunoassay Standard Curves with the StandardCurve Class: Bead Array Example
text: Parameters entire study set named list fitting standard curve influence fit. following parameters accepted values definitions. example uses suggested defaults. applyProzone Logical (TRUE FALSE). Determines whether prozone (hook effect) detection correction applied. TRUE: Detects adjusts potential signal suppression low dilutions. FALSE: prozone correction performed. blank_option Character string. Controls blank wells handled. Options include: "ignored": Blank wells excluded analysis. "subtracted": geometric mean blank responses subtracted observed responses. "subtracted_3x": 3 times geometric mean blank responses subtracted observed responses. "subtracted_10x": 10 times geometric mean blank responses subtracted observed responses. MFI values test samples zero possible (fluorescence intensity physically constrained non-negative values) set zero. is_log_response Logical (TRUE FALSE). Indicates whether response variable (e.g., MFI absorbance) log-transformed. TRUE: Fit models log-transformed response. FALSE: Use raw response values. is_log_independent Logical (TRUE FALSE). Specifies whether independent variable (concentration) log-transformed. TRUE: Use log-scale independent variable. FALSE: Use original scale.
code: study_params <- list(   applyProzone       = TRUE,   blank_option       = "ignored",   is_log_response    = TRUE,   is_log_independent = TRUE )

text: $summarize() computes prints fit statistics, curve characteristics (inflection point, LOD, MDC, RDL, LOQ), tidy parameter table console — including best model name, candidate model comparison (AIC, BIC, RSS), QC metrics. summary fitted model parameters alongside lower upper constraints stored $best_fit$best_parameters data frame, making available reporting downstream analysis. row corresponds model parameter whose definitions follows.
code: sc$summarize() #>  #> ================================================================ #>   Standard Curve Summary #> ================================================================ #>   Best model   : gompertz4 #>   curve_id     : 6 #>   Response var : mfi  |  Independent var: concentration #> Finished tidy.nlsLM #> [compute_inflection_point] Inflection point: (x = 2.959566, y = 2.560958) #> MDC/RDL - mindc: 2.293, maxdc: 4.494, minrdl: 2.516, maxrdl: 4.19 #> ================================================================  sc$best_fit$best_model_name #> [1] "gompertz4" sc$best_fit$best_parameters        # parameter table #>   curve_id term     lower    upper estimate  std_error statistic      p_value #> 1        6    a -4.698970 4.444438 1.387958 0.07557519 18.365257 1.679637e-06 #> 2        6    b  0.100000 2.000000 1.570738 0.21289353  7.378047 3.179050e-04 #> 3        6    c  1.000000 5.477121 2.959566 0.06145124 48.161203 5.372477e-09 #> 4        6    d  1.269523 6.031901 4.576503 0.11186282 40.911746 1.426055e-08 sc$best_fit$best_fit_summary # QC metrics + fit stats (one row) #>   curve_id iter status model_name        a        b        c        d  g #> 1        6    6   TRUE  gompertz4 1.387958 1.570738 2.959566 4.576503 NA #>   inflect_x inflect_y     llod     ulod    mindc    maxdc   minrdl   maxrdl #> 1  2.959566  2.560958 1.572884 4.302785 2.293386 4.494308 2.515882 4.189783 #>       lloq    uloq   lloq_y   uloq_y dfresidual nobs rsquare_fit      aic #> 1 2.346861 3.57245 1.620565 3.564412          6   10   0.9945901 -8.70418 #>         bic  loglik        mse       cv bkg_method is_log_response is_log_x #> 1 -7.191254 9.35209 0.01503357 4.183725    ignored            TRUE     TRUE #>   apply_prozone                                                 formula #> 1          TRUE mfi ~ a + (d - a) * exp(-exp(-b * (concentration - c)))

what: A more advanced example: Fitting Multiple Antigens or Plates
title: Frequentist Modeling of Immunoassay Standard Curves with the StandardCurve Class: Bead Array Example
text: Since bead_assay_example contains two antigens (alpha beta) across three plates, can iterate combinations. Call filter_by_curve_id() new curve_id lookup table pass result $set_data() — replaces data clears downstream state automatically. Key Notes: Pull antigen name lookup table iteration know constraint set apply. assumes curve_id_lookup antigen column. Swap sc$antigen_constraints $set_curve_settings() set_curve_settings() reads antigen_constraints configure model bounds. swap , new constraints take effect next call. $set_data() clears downstream state (fitted models, summaries, etc.) reset antigen_constraints, swap step still necessary persist safely overwritten next iteration. Alternatively, constraint differences substantial want cleaner separation, instantiate fresh StandardCurve object per antigen inside loop using StandardCurve$new() appropriate constraint data frame. avoids risk stale state slightly verbose.
code: # Define per-antigen constraints as a named list antigen_constraints_list <- list(   alpha = data.frame(     antigen                      = "alpha",     l_asy_min_constraint         = 0,     l_asy_max_constraint         = 0,     l_asy_constraint_method      = "default",     standard_curve_concentration = 10000,     pcov_threshold               = 15,     stringsAsFactors             = FALSE   ),   beta = data.frame(     antigen                      = "beta",     l_asy_min_constraint         = 0,     l_asy_max_constraint         = 0,     l_asy_constraint_method      = "default",     standard_curve_concentration = 10000,     pcov_threshold               = 15,     stringsAsFactors             = FALSE   ) )  all_summaries <- vector("list", nrow(bead_assay_example$curve_id_lookup))  for (i in seq_len(nrow(bead_assay_example$curve_id_lookup))) {    cid        <- bead_assay_example$curve_id_lookup$curve_id[i]   antigen    <- bead_assay_example$curve_id_lookup$antigen[i]   # pull antigen name   curve_data <- filter_by_curve_id(bead_assay_example, curve_id = cid)    # Update the StandardCurve object with new data AND new constraints   sc$set_data(curve_data)   sc$antigen_constraints <- antigen_constraints_list[[antigen]]  # swap constraints    sc$set_curve_settings()$fit()$summarize()   all_summaries[[i]] <- sc$get_results()$best_fit_summary }  combined_summary <- do.call(rbind, all_summaries) combined_summary

title: StandardCurve — StandardCurve
text: Wraps full curveRfreq standard-curve fitting pipeline single stateful object. Data loading external — supply pre-loaded data list via $new() $set_data(), call $set_curve_settings(), $fit(), $summarize(), $plot(), $propagate_error() sequence. intermediate results stored public fields can inspected stage.

title: StandardCurve — StandardCurve
text: loaded_data List pre-loaded assay data (standards, blanks, samples, antigen_constraints, response_var, indep_var, ...). Produced externally, e.g. pull_data(). response_var Name response column (e.g. "mfi"). indep_var Name independent variable column (e.g. "concentration"). study_params Named list study-level modelling parameters. Keys: applyProzone, blank_option, is_log_response, is_log_independent. antigen_constraints Data frame per-antigen constraint settings. model_names Character vector candidate model names consider. curve_col Name curve-ID column data. Default "curve_id". curve_id_element_order Character vector defining positional order fields inside curve_id string. is_display_log_response Logical. Display response log scale plots. is_display_log_independent Logical. Display concentration log scale plots. verbose Logical. Print diagnostic messages TRUE. antigen_plate List returned select_antigen_plate(). processed_data List returned preprocess_robust_curves(). formulas List model formulas select_model_formulas(). model_constraints Parameter bounds obtain_model_constraints(). start_lists Multi-start parameter lists make_start_lists(). fit_robust_lm Raw list candidate model fits. fit_summary Data frame summarising candidate fit (AIC, BIC, ...). fit_params Data frame per-model parameter estimates CIs. plot_data List used internally plotting / model comparison. best_fit List returned select_model_fit_AIC() augmented tidy.nlsLM() summarize_fit(). se_antigen_table Standard-error lookup table across antigens. current_se SE values currently selected antigen. .selection_args Internal cache arguments last $set_curve_settings().

title: Compute MDC and RDL values from fitted model and LODs — generate_mdc_rdl
text: best_fit list containing: best_fit fitted model object supporting predict(), coef(), vcov(). best_data data.frame used fitting model. lods list containing llod ulod, typically generate_lods. independent_variable Character string naming independent variable column best_data. verbose Logical; TRUE, prints computed MDC RDL values.

title: Compute MDC and RDL values from fitted model and LODs — generate_mdc_rdl
text: named list : mindc Minimum detectable concentration (fitted curve = LLOD). maxdc Maximum detectable concentration (fitted curve = ULOD). minrdl Minimum reliable detection limit (lower CI = LLOD). maxrdl Maximum reliable detection limit (upper CI = ULOD).

title: Compute MDC and RDL values from fitted model and LODs — generate_mdc_rdl
text: Root-finding performed using uniroot() observed range independent variable. prediction standard error computed using delta method: $$SE(\hat{y}) = \sqrt{\nabla f(\theta)^T V \nabla f(\theta)}$$ \(V\) variance-covariance matrix fitted parameters. Reliable detection limits (RDL) defined using confidence interval: Lower bound: \(\hat{y}(x) - t_{\alpha/2} \cdot SE(x)\) Upper bound: \(\hat{y}(x) + t_{\alpha/2} \cdot SE(x)\) valid root found within observed data range, corresponding value returned NA.

title: Compute MDC and RDL values from fitted model and LODs — generate_mdc_rdl
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ lods <- generate_lods(best_fit) mdc_rdl <- generate_mdc_rdl(best_fit, lods, independent_variable = "log_dilution") } # }

title: Summarize Fitted Model with QC Metrics, LOD, Detection Limits, and Curvature LOQs — summarize_fit
text: function designed operate output model fitting step produce consistent summary structure downstream analysis, QC filtering, reporting. returned summary includes: Model parameters: , b, c, d, g Inflection point coordinates: inflect_x, inflect_y Goodness--fit metrics: residual sum squares (RSS), mean squared error (MSE), R-squared, Akaike information criterion (AIC), Bayesian information criterion (BIC), log-likelihood Convergence diagnostics: iteration count convergence status Limits detection: llod: Lower limit detection ulod: Upper limit detection Detection limits derived fitted curve: mindc, maxdc: Minimum maximum detectable concentrations minrdl, maxrdl: Reliable detection limits based confidence intervals Curvature-based limits quantification (LOQ): lloq, uloq: LOQ values based extrema second derivative lloq_y, uloq_y: Corresponding predicted response values Metadata: source, transformation flags, model formula inflection point computed using compute_inflection_point corresponds location second derivative fitted curve equals zero. Limits detection (LOD) computed using generate_lods, optionally incorporating blank standard error. MDC RDL values computed using generate_mdc_rdl via root-finding observed data range. Curvature-based LOQs computed using compute_loqs, identifies local extrema second derivative fitted curve refines locations using quadratic interpolation. valid model, derivative data, extrema available, corresponding values returned NA.
